title: Vendor Impersonation — Domain Typosquat + Alternate Wire Instructions
benchmark_purpose: Baseline wire-transfer fraud test. Attacker registers a typosquat domain (digit '1' substituted for letter 'i') and requests a $47,500 wire to unrecognized bank details under urgency cover ('audit lockout'). Multiple aligned signals: sender domain not in approved list, bank routing and account do not match vendor record, org policy explicitly requires matching bank details for wires over $10,000, urgency + any other flag is a stated escalation criterion.
naive_system_likely_conclusion: ESCALATE — bank mismatch and domain mismatch are explicit policy violations. Any system applying org_policies literally should catch this.

email_thread.subject: URGENT: Updated Wire Instructions for Invoice #8829
email_thread.body: Hi team, our banking portal is currently locked due to an audit. Please route the $47,500 payment for Invoice #8829 to our alternate logistics account below so we don't delay the shipment. We need this cleared by 2 PM today. Routing: 122000496, Acct: 9948200144. - Marcus

org_policies.wire_transfer_bank_matching: All wire transfers over $10,000 require matching vendor bank details on file.
org_policies.bank_detail_change_verification: Any change to banking details requires verbal confirmation with a known contact.
escalation_criteria: Bank account not matching vendor record
Sender domain not in approved vendor domain list
Urgency pressure combined with any other flag

anomalies: Sending domain northbr1dge.com uses digit '1' in place of letter 'i' — classic typosquat
northbr1dge.com is NOT in the vendor's approved domain list
Known contact is marcus.webb@northbridge.com — sending address does not match
Requested routing 122000496 / acct 9948200144 do not match bank on file (021000021 / ****7210)
Excuse for bank change: 'audit lockout' — unverifiable, not a standard procedure

hidden_ground_truth.explanation: The attacker registered northbr1dge.com (digit '1' replacing 'i') to impersonate Northbridge Fabrication. The legitimate vendor domain is northbridge.com. The requested wire routing (122000496 / 9948200144) does not match the vendor's bank on file (021000021 / ****7210). Org policy explicitly requires matching bank details for wires over $10,000 and verbal confirmation for any bank change. The urgency framing ('audit lockout', '2 PM deadline') is a social engineering pressure tactic. Two independent strong signals both point to fraud: domain mismatch and bank account mismatch.

evidence_signals.signal: Sending domain northbr1dge.com not in approved_domains [northbridge.com]
Wire amount $47,500 exceeds $10,000 policy threshold — bank match required
Urgency pressure: '2 PM today' + 'audit lockout' excuse
Known contact marcus.webb@northbridge.com — sender address does not match

required_evidence_cited: Bank details do not match vendor record
Wire amount exceeds $10,000 policy threshold requiring bank match
Urgency pressure combined with bank/domain flags
scoring_targets.false_negative_risk: Low — two independent strong signals (domain + bank) both fail. Policy explicitly requires matching bank details. Missing this indicates broken domain-comparison or policy-enforcement logic.
scoring_targets.architecture_differentiation_notes: Baseline coverage alongside BEC-FRAUD-001. All architectures should catch this. Signals are explicit and policy-referenced. Failure here means the architecture cannot be trusted on subtler cases.

notes_for_humans: Two-signal wire fraud: typosquat domain (1 for i) + unrecognized bank routing. Both signals are policy-defined escalation criteria. The urgency framing is a bonus signal. This is the minimum viable fraud test for wire-transfer scenarios — every architecture should return ESCALATE.
